sections.paragraph:
-

(1) an ox, or cow, comm. gen., like βοῦς and bos, Germ. Rind. It only occurs in pl. אֲלָפִים Psa 8:8 Pro 14:4 used of a cow, Deu 7:13 28:4 . The singular is found in the name of the first letter, Aleph, Alpha. As to the etymology, see אַלּוּף No. 3 .

(2) a thousand (Arab. أَلْفُ, Syr. ܐܰܠܦܳܐ id.; but Eth. እልፍ፡ signifies ten thousand. Perhaps it is pr. a joining together, and large conjunction of numbers). The nouns enumerated generally follow the numeral, and some indeed in the singular, as אִישׁ Jud. 15:16 others in the plural, 2Sa 10:18 1Ki 10:26 Deu 1:11 others promiscuously, as כִּכָּר 1Ch 19:6, and כִּכָּרִים 29:7 . More rarely, and only in the later Hebrew, does the noun precede, 1Ch 22:14 2Ch 1:6. Comp. Lehrg. p. 695, 697, 699. The principle is different of the phrase אֶלֶף כֶּסֶף “a thousand (shekels) of silver,” as to which see Lehrg. p. 700. It is not unfrequently put for a round number, Job 9:3 33:23 Psa 50:10.-Dual אַלְפַּיִם “two thousands,” Jud. 20:45 1Ch 5:21.-Pl. אֲלָפִים thousands, e.g. שְׁלשׁת אֲלָפִים Exo 38:26. Far more often used of a round number, אַלְפֵי רְבָבָה “thousands of myriads,” Gen 24:60.

(3) a family, i.e. מִשְׁפָּחָה, many of which constituted one tribe (שֵׁבֶט, מַטֶּה), Jud. 6:15 1Sa 10:19 23:23 . Used of a town as the abode of a family, Mic 5:1.

(4) [Eleph], pr.n. a town of the Benjamites, Jos 18:28.
